title: Reading and Use of English – Part 1
description: Read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.

content.text: High in the European Alps, glaciers have long been (0) regarded as timeless monuments of nature. However, recent scientific missions have (1) light on how quickly these icy giants are retreating. Glaciologists, who monitor these changes closely, have (2) alarm bells regarding the pace of the melt. The primary concern is not just the loss of scenic beauty, but the (3) impact on local ecosystems and water supplies. Many rivers that (4) through European valleys depend on glacial meltwater during the dry summer months. If these glaciers continue to disappear, the regions downstream may face a (5) shortage of water for agriculture and hydroelectric power. Interestingly, as the ice thaws, it often (6) ancient artifacts that have been trapped for millennia, providing a unique window into prehistoric life. Scientists are now (7) against time to document these findings before they deteriorate. Despite the grim outlook, some experts believe that immediate global action could still (8) the worst of the damage. For now, the retreating ice stands as a stark reminder of our changing planet, urging us to reconsider our relationship with the natural world and the delicate balance that sustains life across the high mountain ranges of the world.

example.explanation: “Regarded as” is the standard passive structure for opinions. “Thought” or “believed” would require “to be”.

questions.explanation: “Shed light on” is a fixed idiomatic expression meaning to make something clearer.
“Sound the alarm bells” is a fixed phrase meaning to warn of danger.
“Profound impact” is a strong academic collocation for a deep or significant effect.
“Rivers flow” is the natural collocation for the movement of water.
“Severe shortage” is the standard collocation for a serious lack of something.
“Reveals” is used when something previously hidden becomes visible.
“Racing against time” is a fixed idiomatic expression meaning to work fast before a deadline.
“Avert” is the specific verb used for preventing a disaster or negative outcome.
